title: Safe Password Practices: Creating Strong Posters
subtitle: Lock down your data with uncrackable keys!
overview: In this hands-on session, students explore the core mechanics of password strength, learn defensive strategies against cyber-attacks, and design informational campaign posters to spread awareness about safe password practices.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — student-designed posters, password crack-time matrices on the board, and campaign galleries.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Photo of the password matrix comparing length vs crack time on the whiteboard
gallery.caption: Length vs. Complexity matrix — mapping out how quickly hackers crack weak keys!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Visual guide or student prototype sketch of their password security poster
gallery.caption: Poster Blueprint — Bold headlines, simple graphics, and clear security rules.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Diagram showing a robot rapidly trying 10,000 keys in a lock
gallery.caption: Brute Force Attack — A computer guessing passwords at lightning speed!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Photo of students presenting their cybersecurity awareness posters to the class
gallery.caption: Students presenting their completed 'Safe Password' posters during the gallery walk

keyTopics: The Length Rule — Why a longer sentence-based 'Passphrase' beats a short complex password | Length > Complexity
Character Mix — Mixing capital letters (A), lowercase letters (a), numbers (7), and symbols (@#$%) to scramble your code
Brute Force Attack — High-speed software trying millions of random word combinations every single second
Dictionary Attack — Hackers running automated lists of real words and common names to guess lazy credentials
Common Traps — Banishing predictable sequences (123456, qwerty) and public details (birthdays, pet names)
Password Managers — A digital vault that stores and generates uncrackable unique keys for every account
MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) — The double-lock system (e.g., Password + OTP code sent to a phone)
Poster Design Framework — Creating impact using bold titles, catchy slogans, and clear action steps | LOOK LOOK LEARN!

activity.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Poster Campaign
parts: Sample catchphrases (e.g., 'Make it Long, Keep it Strong!', 'Don't Share the Key!')
steps: Brainstorming: Students pick one central rule (e.g., Passphrases, MFA, or Avoiding Pet Names).
Slogan Design: Write a punchy, high-impact title that catches the eye immediately from across the room.
Visual Illustration: Draw a clear symbol of security (like a shield, a multi-layered vault, or a digital dragon guarding a lock).
The 'Rule of 3': Add three bullet-point action steps that any student can read and follow in under 10 seconds.
Gallery Walk: Hang completed posters up in the classroom or school corridors to educate others!

quiz.q: Which of the following passwords takes the longest time for a computer to crack?
options: password123
blue-elephants-dance-together
Monkey!
quiz.explanation: Long Passphrases win! 'blue-elephants-dance-together' is incredibly long, creating billions of geometric mathematical combinations that confound brute-force systems.

options: A hacker physically breaking a computer screen
A program trying millions of character combinations at extreme speed until it hits the right one
Sending fake emails to steal bank accounts
Forgetting your password multiple times in a row
quiz.explanation: Brute Force = Guessing Storm! Automated software guesses codes at lightning speeds. The shorter your password, the faster the storm breaks it.

quiz.q: Why is using your dog's name or your birthday a dangerous password practice?
options: Because it is too long to type out
Because pets can guess your password
Because that public information can be easily searched or guessed by bad actors
Because computers do not like names
quiz.explanation: Social Engineering Trap! Hackers can check public social profiles to find out birthdays, favorite sports, or pet names, making them target number one in a custom dictionary attack.

quiz.q: What does MFA stand for in digital security?
options: Many File Access
Multi-Factor Authentication
Main Frame Allocation
Mobile Flight Assistant
quiz.explanation: MFA = Double Lock! Multi-Factor Authentication requires your password AND another verifying item, like an OTP code sent directly to your phone.

quiz.q: What is the primary goal of our poster project today?
options: To hide our real passwords inside drawings
To create a catchy visual campaign that educates our school on safe password habits
To practice painting landscapes
To build a software application
quiz.explanation: Awareness Campaign! The posters serve as bright, communicative public anchors that quickly teach others how to protect their identities.

quiz.q: How does a Password Manager help a user?
options: It remembers and auto-fills unique, highly complex passwords for all your accounts inside an encrypted vault
It posts your passwords securely online so you don't lose them
It speeds up your internet connection speed
It types out your homework automatically
quiz.explanation: Digital Vault! A password manager safely logs every login detail so you only ever have to memorize one singular master key.

quiz.q: Which character combination creates the strongest password complexity?
options: Only lowercase letters
Only numbers and symbols
A varied mix of capital letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and special symbols
Repeating the same letter multiple times
quiz.explanation: Scrambled Mix! Blending uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols (@#$%) exponentially expands the search space a hacking program has to calculate.
